does: This survey provides a comprehensive account of AI systems for mathematical reasoning, organizing the field along four main dimensions: informal reasoning over text and diagrams (encompassing math word problem solving, multimodal geometry, and vision-language models), formal reasoning in proof assistants (including autoformalization, tactic prediction, and proof search), mathematical discovery (where systems propose constructions or improve bounds on open problems), and inference and training techniques (such as chain-of-thought prompting, tool use, process reward models, and reinforcement learning from verification). The work traces the evolution from early rule-based and template-driven approaches through neural methods to contemporary large language models, neuro-symbolic systems, and integrated multi-agent architectures that combine multiple reasoning modalities.
connects: This survey builds on decades of work in automated theorem proving and symbolic AI, extending classic approaches with modern neural methods. It synthesizes recent advances in language models for mathematical reasoning that began with prompt engineering techniques and progressed through fine-tuning strategies, while also incorporating parallel developments in neuro-symbolic systems that marry neural networks with formal logic. The work draws on earlier research in geometry automation, constraint satisfaction, and program synthesis, while recognizing how large language models have transformed the field by providing stronger baseline capabilities for both informal and formal mathematical reasoning. It situates recent breakthroughs in verified discovery and proof assistant integration within the longer arc of attempts to automate mathematical problem-solving across both human-readable and machine-checkable forms.
enables: This integrated perspective enables future work to recognize complementarities between informal and formal reasoning systems, potentially leading to tighter feedback loops where informal exploration informs formal proof construction and verification feedback guides discovery. The survey points toward hybrid architectures where language models generate conjectures and proof sketches that formal systems refine and verify, creating reliable mathematical AI systems for assisting research. It identifies opportunities for scaling mathematical reasoning through better alignment of training objectives with mathematical discovery goals, improved integration of external tools and proof assistants, and development of systems that can tackle genuinely open problems. By unifying disparate subfields, the work suggests that future breakthroughs will likely emerge from systems that simultaneously advance informal reasoning, formal verification, and mathematical discovery rather than optimizing each in isolation.
